questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most accurate general psychological definition of the term 'personality' as commonly used in sport psychology literature?

options.text: The unique pattern of traits, behaviours and attitudes that characterises an individual
Only the physical appearance and clothing choices of a person
Solely the academic qualifications possessed by an individual
A list of the technical skills required in a sport

questions.explanation: Personality refers to the unique and relatively stable pattern of traits, behaviours, thoughts and attitudes that characterises an individual and distinguishes them from others; in sport psychology it influences motivation, learning, teamwork and competitive response.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Appearance and clothing are only the external surface of a person.
Academic qualifications form only a small part of personality.
Technical sport skills are abilities, not the whole personality.

questions.question_text: Which of the following psychologists is most closely associated with the somatotype classification of personality that links body build to temperamental characteristics?

options.text: William H. Sheldon of Harvard University
Sigmund Freud of Vienna psychoanalysis
Jean Piaget of child developmental psychology
Albert Bandura of social learning theory

questions.explanation: William H. Sheldon is most closely associated with somatotype classification, which links body build to temperament: endomorph (round, relaxed), mesomorph (muscular, assertive) and ectomorph (lean, introverted); it is a classic example of the type approach to personality.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Freud is associated with psychoanalytic theory, not somatotypes.
Piaget is known for cognitive development of children.
Bandura is known for social learning theory.

questions.question_text: Which of the following somatotypes proposed by Sheldon is characterised by a lean, narrow and linear body build with an introverted and sensitive temperament?

options.text: Endomorph
Mesomorph
Ectomorph
Polymorph

questions.explanation: The ectomorph in Sheldon's classification is characterised by a lean, narrow and linear body build with an introverted, sensitive and reserved temperament; by contrast endomorphs are round and relaxed, while mesomorphs are muscular and assertive.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Endomorphs are round and relaxed, not lean and narrow.
Mesomorphs are muscular and assertive, not lean.
Polymorph is not part of Sheldon's three-type scheme.

questions.question_text: Which of the following psychologists is most closely associated with the classification of personality into introvert, extrovert and ambivert types in analytical psychology?

options.text: Carl Gustav Jung
William Sheldon
Ivan Pavlov
Edward Thorndike

questions.explanation: Carl Gustav Jung proposed the classification of personality into introvert (inward-looking), extrovert (outward-looking) and ambivert (mixed) types in his analytical psychology; the concept remains very influential in modern personality assessment and sports psychology.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Sheldon is known for somatotype classification.
Pavlov is known for classical conditioning in learning.
Thorndike is known for the law of effect in learning.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the key behavioural tendency of an extrovert athlete as classified in Jungian personality theory?

options.text: Prefers solitary quiet reflection away from others
Draws energy from outward social interaction and activity
Completely avoids all kinds of social interaction
Is identical in behaviour to every introverted athlete

questions.explanation: An extrovert athlete, in Jungian theory, draws energy from outward social interaction and activity; they are usually sociable, talkative and lively, and often enjoy team environments and competitive situations more than introverts who prefer quieter reflection.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Solitary reflection is typical of introverts, not extroverts.
Complete avoidance of social interaction is more introverted.
Extroverts and introverts have clearly different tendencies.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the widely accepted acronym that represents the Big Five model of personality traits used in modern personality research including sport psychology?

options.text: OCEAN (Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism)
HIGH (Happiness, Intellect, Goodness, Height)
FAST (Force, Agility, Speed, Technique)
FIRE (Fitness, Intensity, Resilience, Endurance)

questions.explanation: The Big Five personality traits are remembered by the acronym OCEAN — Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness and Neuroticism; this model is widely used in personality research including work on athlete personality and team behaviour.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: HIGH is not a recognised personality model acronym.
FAST describes performance, not personality traits.
FIRE refers to fitness qualities, not personality dimensions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines the concept of motivation in the context of sport psychology and coaching practice in competitive environments?

options.text: A physical skill of performing specific sporting techniques
An internal process that initiates, directs and sustains goal-oriented behaviour
The measurement of maximum strength of a specific athlete
The tactical pattern of a team's specific offensive play

questions.explanation: Motivation is an internal process that initiates, directs and sustains goal-oriented behaviour in athletes; it explains why an athlete starts training, how hard they try, which goals they pursue and how long they persist in the face of obstacles and fatigue.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: A physical skill is a technique, not an internal process.
Maximum strength measurement is a fitness test, not motivation.
Tactical patterns are group plays, not individual motivation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes intrinsic motivation as defined in contemporary sport psychology literature for individual athletes?

options.text: Motivation driven purely by external trophies and money
Motivation driven by personal interest, enjoyment and satisfaction in the activity itself
Motivation driven solely by negative fear of punishment
Motivation driven exclusively by orders from the team manager

questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation refers to engaging in an activity because of personal interest, enjoyment and satisfaction inherent in the activity itself, rather than for external rewards; athletes who love their sport for its own sake show this type of motivation strongly.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: External trophies and money describe extrinsic motivation.
Fear of punishment is external, coercive motivation.
Orders from a manager are external, not internally driven.

questions.question_text: Which of the following examples best illustrates extrinsic motivation operating in a young athlete during a competitive sports season?

options.text: Playing football because you genuinely enjoy the game
Practising badminton only to win a scholarship and cash prize
Dancing alone in private just because it is calming
Running regularly because it makes you feel healthy

questions.explanation: Extrinsic motivation involves engaging in an activity to obtain an external reward or avoid an external consequence; practising badminton only to win a scholarship and cash prize exemplifies this, as the motivation lies outside the inherent enjoyment of the activity.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Playing because you enjoy the game is intrinsic motivation.
Dancing just because it calms you is intrinsic motivation.
Running for health feelings is also intrinsic motivation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following motivation techniques is most likely to enhance long-term adherence to training in a young athlete according to sport psychology research?

options.text: Focusing only on external financial rewards for every practice
Using specific, realistic goals along with positive feedback
Threatening punishment for every missed session
Keeping goals vague and very distant in time

questions.explanation: Setting specific, realistic short- and medium-term goals combined with positive, constructive feedback tends to build intrinsic motivation, a sense of progress and competence, and is linked with better long-term adherence to training than external rewards or threats alone.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Relying only on external rewards risks reducing intrinsic motivation.
Threats undermine both intrinsic motivation and wellbeing.
Vague, distant goals provide little short-term direction.

questions.question_text: Match each of Sheldon's somatotypes in Column A with its characteristic body-temperament description in Column B and choose the correct combination from the given options.

column_b.text: Lean, linear body with introverted temperament
Round, soft body with relaxed temperament
Muscular, strong body with assertive temperament
Refers to the genre of a novel or film

questions.explanation: Endomorph — round, soft body with relaxed temperament; mesomorph — muscular, strong body with assertive temperament; ectomorph — lean, linear body with introverted temperament; the genre of a novel or film is only a loose everyday use of 'type', not Sheldon's classification.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Endomorphs are round and soft with relaxed temperament.
Mesomorphs are muscular and assertive as a rule.
Ectomorphs are lean, linear and tend to be introverted.

questions.question_text: Match each Jungian personality type in Column A with the most appropriate behavioural description from Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: Shows both introverted and extroverted features
Draws energy mainly from inner reflection and quiet
Draws energy mainly from outward social activity
Refers to typing characters on a computer keyboard

questions.explanation: Introverts draw energy mainly from inner reflection and quiet; extroverts draw energy mainly from outward social activity; ambiverts show features of both; 'typing' on a keyboard is a loose everyday use of the word type and has nothing to do with Jungian theory.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Introverts are inward-looking by definition.
Extroverts are outward-looking and socially active.
Ambiverts show a mix of both introverted and extroverted features.

questions.question_text: Match each Big Five (OCEAN) personality trait in Column A with its core behavioural description listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: Tendency toward emotional instability and negative affect
Openness to new ideas, creative and curious

questions.explanation: Openness corresponds to curiosity and creativity; conscientiousness to being organised, disciplined and responsible; extraversion to being sociable, outgoing and energetic; and neuroticism to a tendency toward emotional instability and negative affect — the core OCEAN definitions.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Openness involves curiosity and creativity.
Conscientiousness involves discipline and responsibility.
Neuroticism refers to emotional instability tendencies.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about intrinsic motivation in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in sport psychology.
questions.assertion: Athletes high in intrinsic motivation are generally more persistent when facing difficult training sessions in their sport.
questions.reason: Intrinsic motivation is driven by personal interest, enjoyment and a sense of satisfaction in the activity itself.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Personal interest, enjoyment and a sense of satisfaction remain available even when external rewards disappear or drop off; this inner drive sustains effort through difficult training sessions, directly explaining the greater persistence of intrinsically motivated athletes.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly captures the nature of intrinsic motivation.
Both statements align with current sport psychology research.
Internal enjoyment genuinely supports persistence over time.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about personality traits and sport choice and choose the option that best represents their logical connection in sport psychology.
questions.assertion: Extraverted individuals often tend to enjoy team sports such as football, basketball and volleyball more than highly introverted individuals.
questions.reason: Team sports typically involve intense social interaction, communication and shared activity, which match the preferences of extroverts.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Team sports involve intense social interaction, constant communication and shared activity; extroverts typically enjoy and gain energy from such social settings, so they tend to be drawn toward team sports more than highly introverted individuals, explaining the assertion.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason gives a plausible mechanism for sport choice.
Both statements match general personality-sport research.
Social interaction fits the extrovert energy pattern.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason about the use of external rewards in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical connection to athlete motivation.
questions.assertion: Heavy reliance on only external rewards in youth sport may reduce the athlete's intrinsic motivation over time.
questions.reason: When external rewards become the main reason for participation, the inherent enjoyment of the activity itself can be gradually diminished.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: When external rewards dominate, athletes may begin to participate mainly for the rewards and less for the inherent enjoyment of the activity; the inner drive that supported intrinsic motivation can weaken, directly explaining the undermining effect described in the assertion.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly explains the overjustification mechanism.
Both statements align with sport psychology research findings.
External rewards can genuinely weaken intrinsic enjoyment.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages of a simple motivation cycle in a young athlete in the correct chronological order as commonly described in sport psychology literature.

items.text: Athlete experiences an unmet need or goal
Athlete achieves the goal and feels satisfaction
Athlete sets a new, more challenging goal

options.text: P → Q → R → S
Q → P → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → S → Q

questions.explanation: A simple motivation cycle begins with an unmet need or goal, triggers goal-directed behaviour, leads to goal achievement and satisfaction, and finally the athlete often sets a new, more challenging goal; this cycle explains how motivation sustains continued performance.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: An unmet need must precede goal-directed behaviour.
Goal achievement precedes the setting of the next goal.
New goals come after satisfaction from previous goals.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following coaching steps in the correct chronological order when a coach aims to build intrinsic motivation in a group of young athletes over a season.

items.text: Set clear, realistic and personally meaningful goals with each athlete
Establish a supportive, mastery-focused practice environment
Review progress and provide specific, positive feedback
Gradually increase challenge and athlete autonomy

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → S → Q

questions.explanation: A coach first establishes a supportive, mastery-focused environment; then sets clear, realistic and personally meaningful goals with each athlete; reviews progress with specific positive feedback; and finally gradually raises challenge and athlete autonomy to support continued intrinsic motivation.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: A supportive environment precedes detailed goal setting.
Goals precede the review and feedback step.
Challenge and autonomy are best increased gradually over time.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic motivation are two distinct but often co-existing forms of motivation that influence athletes in sport.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Intrinsic and extrinsic motivation are distinct — one driven by inner interest and enjoyment and the other by external rewards — but they commonly co-exist in athletes; most real-world sport participation reflects some mixture of intrinsic enjoyment and external incentives together.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The two types of motivation are conceptually different.
They commonly appear together in real sport contexts.
Many athletes mix enjoyment with external incentives.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram summarising classic personality theories and identify which theorist is associated with classifying individuals into introvert, extrovert and ambivert categories in analytical psychology.

options.text: William Sheldon
Carl Gustav Jung
Ivan Pavlov
Edward Thorndike

questions.explanation: Carl Gustav Jung classified people into introvert, extrovert and ambivert types in his analytical psychology; Sheldon is associated with somatotype classification, Pavlov with classical conditioning, and Thorndike with the law of effect in behavioural learning theory.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Sheldon is the somatotype theorist, not a Jungian type theorist.
Pavlov is a classical conditioning theorist, not a personality theorist.
Thorndike is a learning theorist with the law of effect.
